{{Short description|Oversight of counsellors and psychotherapists}}
'''Supervision''' is used in counselling, psychotherapy, and other [[mental health]] disciplines as well as many other professions engaged in working with people.<ref>Bernard, J. M., & Goodyear, R. K. (2020). ''Fundamentals of Clinical Supervision'' (6th ed.). Pearson.</ref> Supervision may be applied as well to practitioners in somatic disciplines for their preparatory work for patients as well as collateral with patients. Supervision is a replacement instead of formal retrospective inspection, delivering evidence about the skills of the supervised practitioners.

It consists of the practitioner meeting regularly with another professional, not necessarily more senior, but normally with training in the skills of supervision, to discuss casework and other professional issues in a structured way.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Haggerty |first1=Greg |last2=Hilsenroth |first2=Mark J. |date=2011-03-28 |title=The Use of Video in Psychotherapy Supervision |url=https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1752-0118.2011.01232.x |journal=British Journal of Psychotherapy |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=193–210 |doi=10.1111/j.1752-0118.2011.01232.x |issn=0265-9883|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This is often known as clinical or counselling supervision (consultation differs in being optional advice from someone without a supervisor's formal authority). The purpose is to assist the practitioner to learn from his or her experience and progress in expertise, as well as to ensure good service to the client or patient.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Watkins |first=C. Edward |date=2011 |title=Does Psychotherapy Supervision Contribute to Patient Outcomes? Considering Thirty Years of Research |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |journal=The Clinical Supervisor |language=en |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=235–256 |doi=10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |issn=0732-5223|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Learning shall be applied to planning work as well as to diagnostic work and therapeutic work.<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2015 |title=Guidelines for clinical supervision in health service psychology. |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/a0038112 |journal=American Psychologist |volume=70 |issue=1 |pages=33–46 |doi=10.1037/a0038112 |pmid=25581007 |issn=1935-990X |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Derek Milne defined clinical supervision as: "The formal provision, by approved supervisors, of a relationship-based education and training that is work-focused and which manages, supports, develops and evaluates the work of colleague/s".<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Milne |first=Derek |date=2007 |title=An empirical definition of clinical supervision |url=https://doi.org/10.1348/014466507x197415 |journal=British Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=46 |issue=4 |pages=437–447 |doi=10.1348/014466507x197415 |pmid=17535535 |issn=0144-6657|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The main methods that supervisors use are corrective feedback on the supervisee's performance, teaching, and collaborative goal-setting.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ellis |first=Michael V. |date=2010-05-14 |title=Bridging the Science and Practice of Clinical Supervision: Some Discoveries, Some Misconceptions |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/07325221003741910 |journal=The Clinical Supervisor |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=95–116 |doi=10.1080/07325221003741910 |issn=0732-5223}}</ref> It therefore differs from related activities, such as mentoring and coaching, by incorporating an evaluative component. Supervision's objectives are "normative" (e.g. quality control), "restorative" (e.g. encourage emotional processing) and "formative" (e.g. maintaining and facilitating supervisees' competence, capability and general effectiveness).

Some practitioners (e.g. art, music and drama therapists, chaplains, psychologists, and mental health occupational therapists) have used this practice for many years.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hilsenroth |first1=Mark J. |last2=Kivlighan |first2=Dennis M. |last3=Slavin-Mulford |first3=Jenelle |date=2015 |title=Structured supervision of graduate clinicians in psychodynamic psychotherapy: Alliance and technique. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/cou0000058 |journal=Journal of Counseling Psychology |language=en |volume=62 |issue=2 |pages=173–183 |doi=10.1037/cou0000058 |pmid=25602605 |issn=1939-2168|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In other disciplines the practice may be a new concept. For NHS nurses, the use of clinical supervision is expected as part of good practice.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Read The Code online - The Nursing and Midwifery Council |url=https://www.nmc.org.uk/standards/code/read-the-code-online/ |access-date=2023-02-01 |website=www.nmc.org.uk}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.clinical-supervision.com |title=clinical-supervision.com |publisher=clinical-supervision.com |access-date=2012-02-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20000817013154/http://www.clinical-supervision.com/ |archive-date=2000-08-17 }}</ref> In a randomly controlled trial in Australia,<ref>White E, Winstanley J, Does clinical supervision lead to better patient outcomes in mental health nursing? Nursing Times; 106: 16, pp.&nbsp;16–18, 2010.</ref> White and Winstanley looked at the relationships between supervision, quality of nursing care and patient outcomes, and found that supervision had sustainable beneficial effects for supervisors and supervisees. Waskett believes that maintaining the practice of clinical supervision always requires managerial and systemic backing, and has examined the practicalities of introducing and embedding clinical supervision into large organisations such as NHS Trusts (2009, 2010).<ref>Waskett, C. Clinical Supervision using the 4S model 1: considering the structure and setting it up' Nursing Times 106: 16, pp.&nbsp;12–14, 2010.</ref><ref>Waskett C', Clinical Supervision using the 4S model 2: training supervisors to deliver effective sessions' Nursing Times 106: 17, pp.&nbsp;19–21, 2010.</ref><ref>Waskett, C. 'Clinical supervision using the 4S model 3: Keeping it going. Nursing Times 106: 18, 2010.</ref> Clinical supervision has some overlap with managerial activities, mentorship, and preceptorship, though all of these end or become less direct as staff develop into senior and autonomous roles.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Davis |first1=Cynthia |last2=Burke |first2=Linda |date=September 2012 |title=The effectiveness of clinical supervision for a group of ward managers based in a district general hospital: an evaluative study |journal=Journal of Nursing Management |volume=20 |issue=6 |pages=782–793 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2834.2011.01277.x |issn=1365-2834 |pmid=22967296|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Key issues around clinical supervision in healthcare raised have included time and financial investment.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2017-11-27 |title=Nursing theories 2: clinical supervision |url=https://www.nursingtimes.net/roles/nurse-educators/nursing-theories-2-clinical-supervision-27-11-2017/ |access-date=2023-02-01 |website=Nursing Times |language=en}}</ref> It has however been suggested that quality improvement gained, reduced sick leave and burnout, and improved recruitment and retention make the process worthwhile.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Barratt |first=Caroline |date=2017-01-18 |title=Exploring how mindfulness and self-compassion can enhance compassionate care |journal=Nursing Standard |volume=31 |issue=21 |pages=55–63 |doi=10.7748/ns.2017.e10671 |issn=2047-9018 |pmid=28097993}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wallbank |first1=Sonya |last2=Hatton |first2=Sue |date=July 2011 |title=Reducing burnout and stress: the effectiveness of clinical supervision |journal=Community Practitioner: The Journal of the Community Practitioners' & Health Visitors' Association |volume=84 |issue=7 |pages=31–35 |issn=1462-2815 |pmid=21941708}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=White |first=Edward |date=June 2017 |title=Clinical Supervision: invisibility on the contemporary nursing and midwifery policy agenda |journal=Journal of Advanced Nursing |language=en |volume=73 |issue=6 |pages=1251–1254 |doi=10.1111/jan.12970|pmid=27021076 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ashburner |first=C |date=2016 |title=Seeing things differently: Evaluating psychodynamically informed group clinical supervision for general hospital nurses |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/136140960400900108?journalCode=jrna |journal=NT Research|volume=9 |issue=1|pages=38–48 |doi=10.1177/136140960400900108 |s2cid=71035507 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Teasdale |first1=K. |last2=Brocklehurst |first2=N. |last3=Thom |first3=N. |date=January 2001 |title=Clinical supervision and support for nurses: an evaluation study |journal=Journal of Advanced Nursing |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=216–224 |doi=10.1046/j.1365-2648.2001.01656.x |doi-broken-date=17 July 2025 |issn=0309-2402 |pmid=11168705}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cutcliffe |first1=John R. |last2=Sloan |first2=Graham |last3=Bashaw |first3=Marie |date=October 2018 |title=A systematic review of clinical supervision evaluation studies in nursing |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/inm.12443 |journal=International Journal of Mental Health Nursing |language=en |volume=27 |issue=5 |pages=1344–1363 |doi=10.1111/inm.12443|pmid=29446513 |s2cid=46801036 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2017-11-27 |title=Nursing theories 2: clinical supervision |url=https://www.nursingtimes.net/roles/nurse-educators/nursing-theories-2-clinical-supervision-27-11-2017/ |access-date=2023-02-01 |website=Nursing Times |language=en}}</ref>

== United Kingdom ==
Clinical supervision is used in many disciplines in the British [[National Health Service]]. Registered allied health professionals such as [[occupational therapists]],<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.cot.org.uk |title=Occupational therapy - helping you to live life your way &#124; BAOT/COT |publisher=Cot.org.uk |date=2012-01-23 |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> [[physiotherapist]]s,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.csp.org.uk/ |title=The Chartered Society of Physiotherapy &#124; The Chartered Society of Physiotherapy |publisher=Csp.org.uk |date= |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> [[dietician]]s,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.dietetics.org.uk/ |title=British Dietetic Association BDA |publisher=Dietetics.org.uk |date=2011-11-01 |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> [[speech and language therapy|speech and language therapists]]<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.rcslt.org/ |title=Home |publisher=Rcslt.org |date= |access-date=2012-02-10}}</ref> and [[art therapy|art]],<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.baat.org/ |title=British Association of Art Therapists |publisher=Baat.org |date= |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> [[music therapy|music]] and [[drama therapy|drama therapists]] are now expected to have regular clinical supervision. C. Waskett (2006) has written on the application of [[solution focused brief therapy|solution focused]] supervision skills to either counselling or clinical supervision work. Practising members of the British Association for Counselling and Psychotherapy<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.bacp.co.uk |title=The British Association for Counselling & Psychotherapy |publisher=BACP |date= |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> are bound to have supervision for at least 1.5 hours a month. Students and trainees must have it at a rate of one hour for every eight hours of client contact.

The concept is also well used in psychology, social work, the probation service and at other workplaces.

==Models or approaches==
There are many different ways of developing supervision skills which can be helpful to the clinician or practitioner in their work.<ref name=":0">Bernard, J. M., & Goodyear, R. K. (2020). ''Fundamentals of Clinical Supervision'' (6th ed.). Pearson.</ref> Specific models or approaches to both counselling supervision and clinical supervision come from different historical strands of thinking and beliefs about relationships between people. A few examples are given below.

Peter Hawkins (1985<ref>Hawkins, P. (1985). Humanistic psychotherapy supervision: a conceptual framework. Self and Society: Journal of Humanistic Psychology, 13(2), 69–79.</ref>) developed an integrative process model which is used internationally in a variety of helping professions. His "Seven Eyed model of Supervision" was further developed by Peter Hawkins along with Robin Shohet, Judy Ryde and Joan Wilmot in "Supervision in the Helping Professions" (1989, 2000 and 2006 and 2012<ref>Hawkins, P., and Shohet, R. (2012). Supervision in the Helping Professions. (Fourth edition) Maidenhead: Open University Press/ McGraw Hill.</ref>) and with Nick Smith in "Coaching, Mentoring and organisational Consultancy: Supervision and Development" (2006 and 2013<ref>Hawkins, P. and Smith N. (2006, second edition 2013) "Coaching, Mentoring and Organizational Consultancy: Supervision and Development." Maidenhead:  Open University Press/McGraw Hill.</ref>) and is taught on the courses of the Centre for Supervision and Team Development<ref>{{Cite web |title=CSTD |url=https://www.cstd.co.uk/ |access-date=2025-03-01 |website=www.cstd.co.uk}}</ref> as well as many other supervision training courses.

S. Page and V. Wosket describe a cyclical structure.{{Citation needed|date=March 2025}}

F. Inskipp and B. Proctor (1993, 1995) developed an approach based on the normative, formative and restorative elements of the relationship between supervisor and supervisee. The Brief Therapy practice<ref>{{cite web |author=Netrix Limited |url=http://www.brieftherapy.org.uk |title=BRIEF - Europe's largest provider of solution focused Brief Therapy training |website=Brief Therapy |access-date=2012-02-10 }}</ref> teaches a solution focused approach based on the work of [[Steve de Shazer]] and [[Insoo Kim Berg]] which uses the concepts of respectful curiosity, the preferred future, recognition of strengths and resources, and the use of scaling to assist the practitioner to progress (described in <ref>{{cite web |url=http://northwestsolutions.co.uk/sf-supervision-res.html |title=Solution focused supervision article |website=Northwest Solutions |access-date=2012-02-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080830020118/http://www.northwestsolutions.co.uk/sf-supervision-res.html |archive-date=2008-08-30 }}</ref>). Waskett has described teaching solution-focused supervision skills to a variety of professionals<ref>Henderson, P. (Ed.), Supervisor Training: issues and approaches; Waskett; Learning to supervise using a solution focused approach. Karnac, 2009.</ref>

Evidence-based CBT supervision is a distinctive and recent model that is based on cognitive-behaviour therapy (CBT), enhanced by relevant theories (e.g. experiential learning theory), expert consensus statements, and on applied research findings (Milne & Reiser, 2017). It is therefore an example of [[evidence-based practice]], applied to supervision. CBT supervision meets the general definition of clinical supervision, adding some distinctive features that reflect CBT as a therapy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Milne |first=Derek |date=2007 |title=An empirical definition of clinical supervision |url=https://doi.org/10.1348/014466507x197415 |journal=British Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=46 |issue=4 |pages=437–447 |doi=10.1348/014466507x197415 |pmid=17535535 |issn=0144-6657|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This includes a high degree of session structure and direction (e.g. detailed agenda-setting), but within a fundamentally collaborative relationship. Also, there is a primary emphasis on cognitive case conceptualization, mainly through the use of case discussion, intended to develop diagrammatic CBT formulations. But discussion should properly be combined with other CBT techniques, including Socratic questioning, guided discovery, educational role-play, behavioural rehearsal, and corrective feedback. Another distinctive aspect is a focus on evidence-based principles and methods, including the use of reliable instruments for feedback and evaluation, in relation to both therapy and supervision. Perhaps the single most defining characteristic of evidence-based CBT supervision is the active and routine commitment to research methods and findings: where other approaches refer to theory and clinical/supervisory experience for guidance, evidence-based CBT supervision appeals ultimately to 'the data'. Examples of the use of relevant theories, expert consensus statements and research, together with six formally-developed supervision guidelines (illustrated through video clips), can be found in Milne & Reiser (2017).

[[Practice (learning method)|Deliberate practice]] supervision is a focused and structured approach where therapists continuously work on refining specific skills through targeted exercises and feedback.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nurse |first1=Karina |last2=O’shea |first2=Melissa |last3=Ling |first3=Mathew |last4=Castle |first4=Nathan |last5=Sheen |first5=Jade |date=2024-01-31 |title=The influence of deliberate practice on skill performance in therapeutic practice: A systematic review of early studies |journal=Psychotherapy Research |volume=35 |issue=3 |language=en |pages=353–367 |doi=10.1080/10503307.2024.2308159 |issn=1050-3307|doi-access=free |pmid=38295223 }}</ref> Supervisors help identify areas for improvement, set clear objectives, and provide real-time, constructive feedback.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Vaz |first1=Alexandre |last2=McLeod |first2=Julia |last3=Nissen-Lie |first3=Helene A. |date=2025-02-19 |title=Rethinking Psychotherapy Training and Supervision: The Case for Deliberate Practice |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jclp.23777 |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=81 |issue=6 |pages=393–398 |language=en |doi=10.1002/jclp.23777 |pmid=39970009 |issn=0021-9762|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Based on the work of [[K. Anders Ericsson]],<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ericsson |first1=K. Anders |last2=Charness |first2=Neil |date=1994 |title=Expert performance: Its structure and acquisition. |url=https://doi.org/10.1037//0003-066x.49.8.725 |journal=American Psychologist |volume=49 |issue=8 |pages=725–747 |doi= 10.1037/0003-066x.49.8.725|issn=|url-access=subscription }}</ref> deliberate practice supervision emphasizes repetitive practice and reflection to enhance clinical effectiveness and adaptability, ultimately aiming to bridge the gap between current capabilities and desired performance levels in therapeutic settings.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Levenson |first1=Hanna |title=Exercise 11. Using supervision to recognize reenactments. |date=2023 |work=Deliberate practice in psychodynamic psychotherapy. |pages=157–166 |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/0000351-013 |access-date=2025-02-16 |place=Washington |publisher=American Psychological Association |isbn=978-1-4338-3673-2 |last2=Gay |first2=Volney |last3=Binder |first3=Jeffrey L.|doi=10.1037/0000351-013 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> [https://sentio.org/dpresearch Over 20 peer-reviewed empirical studies] have examined the process and outcome of deliberate practice supervision.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nurse |first1=Karina |last2=O’shea |first2=Melissa |last3=Ling |first3=Mathew |last4=Castle |first4=Nathan |last5=Sheen |first5=Jade |date=2024-01-31 |title=The influence of deliberate practice on skill performance in therapeutic practice: A systematic review of early studies |journal=Psychotherapy Research |volume=35 |issue=3 |language=en |pages=353–367 |doi=10.1080/10503307.2024.2308159 |issn=1050-3307|doi-access=free |pmid=38295223 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mahon |first=Daryl |date=2023 |title=A scoping review of deliberate practice in the acquisition of therapeutic skills and practices |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/capr.12601 |journal=Counselling and Psychotherapy Research |language=en |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=965–981 |doi=10.1002/capr.12601 |issn=1473-3145|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Research on Deliberate Practice |url=https://sentio.org/dpresearch |access-date=2025-03-01 |website=Sentio University |language=en-US}}</ref> A review published in 2024 described two major models of deliberate practice supervision.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Clements-Hickman |first1=Alyssa L. |last2=Harris |first2=Kevin R. |date=2024 |title=Deliberate practice for psychotherapy skills: Recommendations and implications based on the state of the science. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/pro0000592 |journal=Professional Psychology: Research and Practice |language=en |volume=55 |issue=6 |pages=563–572 |doi=10.1037/pro0000592 |issn=1939-1323|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The Better Results model, created by Scott Miller, Mark Hubble, and Daryl Chow, uses data from [[Feedback informed treatment|Feedback Informed Treatment]] to guide deliberate practice supervision.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Miller |first1=Scott D. |last2=Chow |first2=Daryl |date=2020 |title=Better results: using deliberate practice to improve therapeutic effectiveness |publisher=American Psychological Association |jstor=j.ctv1chrrmr|isbn=978-1-4338-3190-4 }}</ref>  The Sentio Supervision Model, created by the Sentio Marriage and [[Family therapy|Family Therapy]] MA program in California,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=McGrath |first=Kathryn |date=2024-04-17 |title=President's column |journal=New Zealand Science Review |volume=66 |issue=2 |pages=59 |doi=10.26686/nzsr.v66.9420 |issn=2624-277X|doi-access=free }}</ref> systematically integrates psychotherapy skill building with the use of clinical videos and outcome data to increase trainees' clinical competence and confidence.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Belik |first1=Olga |title=Instructions for the interpersonal psychotherapy deliberate practice exercises. |date=2025 |work=Deliberate practice in interpersonal psychotherapy. |pages=29–32 |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/0000426-002 |access-date=2025-02-16 |place=Washington |publisher=American Psychological Association |isbn=978-1-4338-4046-3 |last2=Schultz |first2=Jessica M. |last3=Fairhurst |first3=Scott |last4=Stuart |first4=Scott |last5=Vaz |first5=Alexandre |last6=Rousmaniere |first6=Tony|doi=10.1037/0000426-002 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Husby |first=Vidar M. |date=2025-02-12 |title=Challenge and Support: Scaffolding the Practicing Therapist in DP Supervision |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jclp.23774 |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=81 |issue=5 |pages=366–378 |language=en |doi=10.1002/jclp.23774 |pmid=39937956 |issn=0021-9762|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Developmental models of supervision view supervisees as progressing through distinct stages of professional growth, requiring different types of supervision at each stage. Stoltenberg & Delworth’s Integrated Developmental Model (IDM) proposes three levels of supervisee development (beginner, intermediate, advanced), each with increasing autonomy and complexity in clinical skills.<ref>Stoltenberg, C. D., & Delworth, U. (1987). ''Supervising Counselors and Therapists: A Developmental Approach''. Jossey-Bass.</ref> Loganbill, Hardy, & Delworth's developmental model describes cycles of stagnation, confusion, and integration as supervisees develop.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Loganbill |first1=Carol |last2=Hardy |first2=Emily |last3=Delworth |first3=Ursula |date=1982 |title=Supervision: A Conceptual Model |url=https://doi.org/10.1177/0011000082101002 |journal=The Counseling Psychologist |volume=10 |issue=1 |pages=3–42 |doi=10.1177/0011000082101002 |issn=0011-0000|url-access=subscription }}</ref> 

In the Discrimination Model of supervision by Janine Bernard, supervisors take on three roles: Teacher (instructing and guiding), Counselor (helping with emotional reactions), and Consultant (collaborative problem-solving), and focus on three skill areas: Process (e.g., interpersonal dynamics), Conceptualization (understanding client issues), and Personalization (how the therapist uses themselves in therapy).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=BERNARD |first=JANINE M. |date=1979 |title=Supervisor Training: A Discrimination Model |url=https://doi.org/10.1002/j.1556-6978.1979.tb00906.x |journal=Counselor Education and Supervision |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=60–68 |doi=10.1002/j.1556-6978.1979.tb00906.x |issn=0011-0035|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>Bernard, J. M., & Goodyear, R. K. (2020). ''Fundamentals of Clinical Supervision'' (6th ed.). Pearson.</ref>

==Research on the effectiveness of supervision==
Some studies have suggested that supervision may improve clinical effectiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bambling |first1=Matthew |last2=King |first2=Robert |last3=Raue |first3=Patrick |last4=Schweitzer |first4=Robert |last5=Lambert |first5=Warren |date=2006 |title=Clinical supervision: Its influence on client-rated working alliance and client symptom reduction in the brief treatment of major depression |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/10503300500268524 |journal=Psychotherapy Research |language=en |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=317–331 |doi=10.1080/10503300500268524 |issn=1050-3307|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Callahan |first1=Jennifer L. |last2=Almstrom |first2=Christina M. |last3=Swift |first3=Joshua K. |last4=Borja |first4=Susan E. |last5=Heath |first5=Christopher J. |date=2009 |title=Exploring the contribution of supervisors to intervention outcomes. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0014294 |journal=Training and Education in Professional Psychology |language=en |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=72–77 |doi=10.1037/a0014294 |issn=1931-3926|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Reese |first1=Robert J. |last2=Usher |first2=Ellen L. |last3=Bowman |first3=Derek C. |last4=Norsworthy |first4=Larry A. |last5=Halstead |first5=Jackie L. |last6=Rowlands |first6=Steven R. |last7=Chisholm |first7=Rachael R. |date=2009 |title=Using client feedback in psychotherapy training: An analysis of its influence on supervision and counselor self-efficacy. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0015673 |journal=Training and Education in Professional Psychology |language=en |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=157–168 |doi=10.1037/a0015673 |issn=1931-3926|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Other studies have raised questions about the effectiveness of supervision.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rousmaniere |first1=Tony G. |last2=Babins-Wagner |first2=Robbie |last3=Swift |first3=Joshua K. |last4=Whipple |first4=Jason |last5=Berzins |first5=Sandy |date=2014 |title=Supervisor Effects on Psychotherapy Outcome in Routine Practice |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/e538742014-001 |access-date=2025-02-21 |website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e538742014-001 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Whipple |first1=Jason |last2=Hoyt |first2=Tyler |last3=Rousmaniere |first3=Tony |last4=Swift |first4=Joshua |last5=Pedersen |first5=Tyler |last6=Worthen |first6=Vaughn |date=2020 |title=Supervisor Variance in Psychotherapy Outcome in Routine Practice: A Replication |journal=SAGE Open |language=en |volume=10 |issue=1 |doi=10.1177/2158244019899047 |issn=2158-2440|doi-access=free }}</ref> Three literature reviews of research in this topic raised concerns about the reliability of these findings and voiced caution in assuming that supervision may improve clinical effectiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ladany |first1=Nicholas |last2=Hill |first2=Clara E. |last3=Corbett |first3=Maureen M. |last4=Nutt |first4=Elizabeth A. |date=1996 |title=Nature, extent, and importance of what psychotherapy trainees do not disclose to their supervisors. |url=https://doi.org/10.1037//0022-0167.43.1.10 |journal=Journal of Counseling Psychology |volume=43 |issue=1 |pages=10–24 |doi=10.1037/0022-0167.43.1.10 |issn=0022-0167|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Beutler |first1=Larry E. |last2=Myeisha |first2=Howard |date=2003 |title=Training in psychotherapy: Why supervision does not work |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/e533232009-003 |access-date=2025-02-21 |website=PsycEXTRA Dataset|doi=10.1037/e533232009-003 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Watkins |first=C. Edward |date=2011 |title=Does Psychotherapy Supervision Contribute to Patient Outcomes? Considering Thirty Years of Research |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |journal=The Clinical Supervisor |language=en |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=235–256 |doi=10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |issn=0732-5223|url-access=subscription }}</ref>   

==Training in supervision==
Counselling or clinical supervisors will be experienced in their discipline and normally then have further training in any of the above-mentioned approaches, or others.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Watkins |first=C. Edward |date=2011 |title=Does Psychotherapy Supervision Contribute to Patient Outcomes? Considering Thirty Years of Research |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |journal=The Clinical Supervisor |language=en |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=235–256 |doi=10.1080/07325223.2011.619417 |issn=0732-5223|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The guidelines of the [[American Psychological Association]],<ref>American Psychological Association. (2014). Guidelines for Clinical Supervision in Health Service Psychology. Retrieved from <nowiki>http://apa.org/about/policy/guidelines-supervision.pdf</nowiki></ref> [[American Counseling Association]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=CACREP SUPERVISOR QUALIFICATIONS |date=24 July 2015 |url=https://www.cacrep.org/section-3-professional-practice/}}</ref> and American Association for Marriage and Family Therapists<ref>{{Cite web |title=Responsibilities and Guidelines for AAMFT Approved Supervisors and Supervisor Candidates |url=https://www.aamft.org/AAMFT/Supervision/Responsibilities.aspx}}</ref> provide standards for supervisor competence and training. There are many different ways of developing supervision skills which can be helpful to the clinician or practitioner in their work.<ref name=":0" /> Training programs in psychology, counseling, social work, and other allied fields often provide graduate-level coursework in supervision. Post-graduate supervisor training is also offered, often by non-profit organizations. For example, the non-profit Sentio Counseling Center offers a one-year Deliberate Practice supervisor training program <ref>Stuart, M. (2024, April). A supervisor’s deliberate practice journey. ''Psychotherapy Bulletin, 59''(2). 8-11.</ref><ref>Levenson, H. (2024, May). What deliberate practice supervision has to offer traditional supervision: Nine take-home messages. ''Psychotherapy Bulletin, 59''(3) 55-59.</ref> that provides over 50 hours of video-based training in Deliberate Practice supervision methods, aiming to enhance supervisory skills through close mentorship with experienced trainers.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Husby |first=Vidar M. |date=2025-02-12 |title=Challenge and Support: Scaffolding the Practicing Therapist in DP Supervision |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jclp.23774 |journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology |volume=81 |issue=5 |pages=366–378 |language=en |doi=10.1002/jclp.23774 |pmid=39937956 |issn=0021-9762|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

* The works of [[Lawrence Shulman]]
* [[Behavioral psychotherapy]]

=== Sources ===
{{refbegin}}
* C Waskett. The SF Journey, in ''Therapy Today'', March 2006, Vol.&nbsp;17, No.&nbsp;2, pp.&nbsp;40–42.
* P Hawkins, R Shohet. ''Supervision in the Helping Professions: an organisational, group and organisational approach''; Open University Press, Maidenhead, 2nd ed 2000 3rd Edition 2006, 4th edition 2012
* P Hawkins and N Smith(2006, second edition 2013) "Coaching, Mentoring and Organizational Consultancy: Supervision and Development." Maidenhead:  Open University Press/McGraw Hill. 
* S Page, V Wosket. ''Supervising the Counsellor: a cyclical model''; Routledge, London & New York, 1995
* F Inskipp, B Proctor. ''The Art, craft and tasks of Counselling supervision'', Part 1 – making the most of supervision, Cascade Publications 1993, and Part 2 – becoming a supervisor, 1995
* C Waskett. [https://web.archive.org/web/20120415135020/http://www.northwestsolutions.co.uk/sf-supervision-res.php The pluses of solution-focused supervision], in ''Healthcare Counselling and Psychotherapy Journal'', Vol.&nbsp;6, No.&nbsp;1.
* C Waskett. [https://web.archive.org/web/20120227033119/http://www.northwestsolutions.co.uk/4s-article-res.php An Integrated Approach to introducing and maintaining supervision: the 4S model]. Nursing Times,105:17, 24–26.
* D Milne (2007). An empirical definition of clinical supervision. British Journal of Clinical Psychology, 46, 437–447.
* D Milne & R Reiser (2017). A manual for evidence-based CBT supervision. Chichester: Wiley Blackwell.
{{refend}}
